# Code Review — 2026-06-17

Scope: full `symaira-corekit` Go shared library (8 packages, ~2.5k LOC tests).
Evidence base: README, `go.mod`, `Makefile`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, every package
source file, and `go test -cover` (62–100% per package). CI already runs race tests,
`go vet`, `gofmt`, and `govulncheck` across linux/macOS — the foundations are solid, so the
items below target correctness/consistency footguns rather than missing infrastructure.

- [ ] **[Security] Bound the update-check HTTP response body**
  - **Status quo:** `fetchLatestRelease` decodes the GitHub response with `json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(...)` and no size cap (`updatecheck/updatecheck.go:177`). The default `http.Client` also follows redirects, while the `#nosec G107` justification at `updatecheck/updatecheck.go:160` only holds for the original GitHub host. A compromised, MITM'd, or redirected endpoint can stream an unbounded body into a CLI that runs the check, exhausting memory. No `io.LimitReader`/`MaxBytesReader` exists anywhere in the repo.
  - **Proposed solution:** Wrap the body in `io.LimitReader(resp.Body, 1<<20)` before decoding (a release JSON is a few KB), mirroring the 1 MB cap already enforced in `mcpserver`. Optionally set `http.Client.CheckRedirect` to keep redirects on `api.github.com`/`github.com` so the `#nosec` justification stays valid.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / high impact

- [ ] **[Security] Use the library's own `SafeMkdirAll` when opening the SQLite DB**
  - **Status quo:** `sqlitekit.Open` creates the database directory with `os.MkdirAll(dir, 0700)` (`sqlitekit/sqlitekit.go:19`), which silently follows pre-existing symlinks. The repo ships a hardened `fsutil.SafeMkdirAll` (`fsutil/safewrite.go:112`) built specifically to reject attacker-placed symlinks during traversal, but the SQLite path bypasses it — weakening the very guarantee the helper provides for user-writable data dirs.
  - **Proposed solution:** Replace the `os.MkdirAll` call with `fsutil.SafeMkdirAll(dir, 0700)` so DB directory creation inherits the symlink-traversal hardening, keeping the safety primitive applied consistently.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact

- [ ] **[UX/DX] Fix the README quickstart — `logkit.Setup` does not exist**
  - **Status quo:** The only usage example in the README calls `logkit.Setup("myapp")` (`README.md:36`), but `logkit` exposes no `Setup` symbol — only `Default`, `NewFromEnv`, and `New` (`logkit/logkit.go`). The first thing a new consumer copies fails to compile, undermining the "drop-in foundation" framing.
  - **Proposed solution:** Update the snippet to a real call, e.g. `logger := logkit.NewFromEnv("myapp")`, and show the `SYM<APP>_LOG_LEVEL` / `_LOG_FORMAT` env vars the function reads.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / high impact

- [ ] **[UX/DX] `logkit.Default()` is hardwired to the `symvault` env prefix**
  - **Status quo:** `Default()` lazily builds the logger via `NewFromEnv("symvault")` (`logkit/logkit.go:27`), so any consumer that calls `logkit.Default()` from `symmemory`/`symseek`/`symfetch` silently reads `SYMVAULT_LOG_LEVEL`/`SYMVAULT_LOG_FORMAT` instead of its own. This contradicts the README's "domain-free / standalone-first" contract (`README.md:7-16`) by baking one product's name into a shared default.
  - **Proposed solution:** Make the default app name configurable — e.g. a `logkit.SetDefaultApp(name)` / `InitDefault(name)` called once at startup, or drop the convenience `Default()` and steer consumers to `NewFromEnv(appName)`. At minimum, document the hardcoded prefix so the footgun is explicit.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact

- [ ] **[Architecture] Stop silently swallowing TOML type-conversion errors in configkit**
  - **Status quo:** In the file-merge path, `applyMapToStruct` calls `setFromInterface(...)` but discards its returned `error` at `configkit/configkit.go:183` and `configkit/configkit.go:191`. A TOML value of the wrong type (e.g. a string where an int is expected) is ignored and the field keeps its default — yet the env-override path (`applyEnvToFields`) does propagate the same conversion errors. Misconfigured config files therefore fail silently and inconsistently.
  - **Proposed solution:** Propagate the `setFromInterface` error up through `applyMapToStruct` → `mergeFile` → `loadOnce` (return it, wrapped with the field/file context) so a malformed TOML value surfaces as a load error, matching the env path's behavior.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Medium effort / medium impact

- [ ] **[Architecture] De-duplicate `AtomicWriteFile` across the platform files**
  - **Status quo:** `AtomicWriteFile` is copied almost verbatim into `fsutil/safewrite.go:15` and `fsutil/safewrite_windows.go:18`; the temp-create → chmod → write → sync → close → cleanup-on-error sequence is identical and only the final rename differs (Windows adds retry/backoff). Any fix to the write/cleanup path must be made twice, inviting drift.
  - **Proposed solution:** Extract the shared body into one cross-platform helper (e.g. `writeTempThenRename(path, data, perm, rename func(tmp, dst) error)`) and keep only the platform-specific `rename` in the build-tagged files.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact

- [ ] **[Performance] Cap line length in the MCP line-delimited transport**
  - **Status quo:** The framed transport bounds `Content-Length` to `1<<20` (`mcpserver/mcpserver.go:193`), but the newline-delimited path reads whole lines via `br.ReadString('\n')` in `readNonEmptyLine` (`mcpserver/mcpserver.go:209`) and `parseLineRequest` with no length limit. A client that sends a very large line without a newline buffers it entirely into memory — an unbounded-allocation asymmetry versus the framed path.
  - **Proposed solution:** Enforce a matching ceiling on line mode — e.g. read through a `bufio.Scanner`/`io.LimitReader` with a max token size (reuse the same 1 MB constant) and return a parse error past the limit instead of buffering unbounded input.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact

## Conclusion

The library is well-tested and the CI/security tooling is already in good shape; the highest-value work is closing small correctness and consistency gaps. **Best first quick win:** fix the README quickstart (`logkit.Setup` → `logkit.NewFromEnv`) — a one-line change that stops the first example a new consumer copies from failing to compile. Pair it with the `io.LimitReader` cap in `updatecheck` for the strongest low-effort security gain.
